data.title: ATI Aerospace And Defense Growth Tests High-Margin Earnings Story
data.description:

  • Management pointed to proprietary alloys as a key factor in higher margins and a shift in product mix.

ATI enters this update with its shares at $143.93 and a very large 5 year return, reflecting substantial compounding over that period. Over the past year the stock is up 135.8%, including gains of 7.8% over the past week and 15.7% over the past month, while year to date performance sits at 20.7%. These moves frame the latest quarter as one more data point investors can weigh against an already strong share price run.

The company is putting clear emphasis on defense related growth, longer term customer commitments, and higher margin proprietary products. For you as an investor, the key questions from here are how durable that demand in aerospace and defense proves to be, and how much further ATI can shift its mix toward these higher margin offerings in coming quarters.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for ATI by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on ATI.

2 things going right for ATI that this headline doesn't cover.

For you as an investor, the key takeaway from this update is the tension between ATI’s product mix story and its near term profit trends. Fourth quarter sales were roughly flat year on year at US$1,177.1 million versus US$1,172.7 million, yet net income in that period moved from US$137.1 million to US$96.6 million, with diluted EPS from continuing operations at US$0.69 compared to US$0.94. Over the full year, however, ATI reported higher sales of US$4,587.4 million versus US$4,362.1 million and higher net income of US$404.3 million compared to US$367.8 million, which suggests the broader direction for the year was positive even though the final quarter was softer on earnings.

How This Fits Into The ATI Narrative

  • The focus on proprietary alloys, next generation jet engines and long term aerospace and defense agreements ties directly to the narrative that higher margin, contracted volumes can support more stable earnings across cycles.
  • The fourth quarter profit step down, despite resilient sales, is a reminder that execution on efficiency and capital intensive projects is not guaranteed to be smooth, which tests the thesis that margin strength alone will carry results.
  • The latest buyback tranche completion, and the pause in repurchases in late 2025, touches on capital allocation and balance sheet flexibility, an angle that is not fully explored in the existing narrative around contracts and capacity investments.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for ATI to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Reported fourth quarter net income and EPS were lower than the prior year period, which shows that higher margin products do not always translate into quarter on quarter earnings strength.
  • ⚠️ ATI relies heavily on aerospace and defense demand, and on a relatively concentrated set of customers, so shifts in ordering patterns or program timing at major peers like Howmet Aerospace or Precision Castparts could affect volumes.
  • 🎁 Full year sales and net income were higher than the previous year, supported by demand in next generation jet engines and missile programs, which reinforces the role of these end markets as important earnings drivers.
  • 🎁 Expanded long term agreements tied to proprietary alloys may give ATI better volume visibility and pricing power versus peers such as Carpenter Technology and Allegheny based competitors that lack the same contract depth.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track how quickly ATI converts its contract wins and proprietary alloy positioning into consistent quarterly earnings, not just full year gains. That includes watching future commentary on capacity expansion, cost efficiency and any updates on aerospace and defense program ramps. It is also worth keeping an eye on capital allocation, including whether share repurchases resume and how that interacts with debt and investment needs.

data.title: Is Gruma. de (BMV:GRUMAB) A Risky Investment?
data.description:
The external fund manager backed by Berkshire Hathaway's Charlie Munger, Li Lu, makes no bones about it when he says 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' When we think about how risky a company is, we always like to look at its use of debt, since debt overload can lead to ruin. We can see that Gruma, S.A.B. de C.V. (BMV:GRUMAB) does use debt in its business. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Generally speaking, debt only becomes a real problem when a company can't easily pay it off, either by raising capital or with its own cash flow. If things get really bad, the lenders can take control of the business. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. By replacing dilution, though, debt can be an extremely good tool for businesses that need capital to invest in growth at high rates of return. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

What Is Gruma. de's Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Gruma. de had debt of US$1.41b at the end of September 2025, a reduction from US$1.47b over a year. However, it does have US$455.6m in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about US$956.7m.

How Strong Is Gruma. de's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Gruma. de had liabilities of US$897.8m due within 12 months, and liabilities of US$1.95b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of US$455.6m as well as receivables valued at US$762.2m due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by US$1.63b.

This deficit isn't so bad because Gruma. de is worth US$6.94b, and thus could probably raise enough capital to shore up its balance sheet, if the need arose. But we definitely want to keep our eyes open to indications that its debt is bringing too much risk.

Check out our latest analysis for Gruma. de

In order to size up a company's debt relative to its earnings, we calculate its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) divided by its interest expense (its interest cover). The advantage of this approach is that we take into account both the absolute quantum of debt (with net debt to EBITDA) and the actual interest expenses associated with that debt (with its interest cover ratio).

With net debt sitting at just 0.90 times EBITDA, Gruma. de is arguably pretty conservatively geared. And it boasts interest cover of 8.2 times, which is more than adequate. Gruma. de's EBIT was pretty flat over the last year, but that shouldn't be an issue given the it doesn't have a lot of debt. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But it is future earnings, more than anything, that will determine Gruma. de's ability to maintain a healthy balance sheet going forward. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

Finally, a business needs free cash flow to pay off debt; accounting profits just don't cut it. So the logical step is to look at the proportion of that EBIT that is matched by actual free cash flow. During the last three years, Gruma. de produced sturdy free cash flow equating to 67% of its EBIT, about what we'd expect. This free cash flow puts the company in a good position to pay down debt, when appropriate.

Our View

The good news is that Gruma. de's demonstrated ability to convert EBIT to free cash flow delights us like a fluffy puppy does a toddler. And the good news does not stop there, as its net debt to EBITDA also supports that impression! Looking at all the aforementioned factors together, it strikes us that Gruma. de can handle its debt fairly comfortably. On the plus side, this leverage can boost shareholder returns, but the potential downside is more risk of loss, so it's worth monitoring the balance sheet. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. data.title: PG&E Storm Readiness Offers Fresh Insight Into Risk And Valuation
data.description:

  • The company has activated its Emergency Operations Center and developed response plans aimed at managing potential power outages and service disruptions.
  • These steps are focused on customer safety, grid reliability, and managing weather-related and wildfire-related risks during the upcoming storm period.

PG&E is a large regulated electric and gas utility serving millions of customers in California, a region that regularly faces severe weather, drought, and wildfire risks. Recent years have kept investor attention on how utilities handle grid hardening, vegetation management, and emergency response in areas with elevated climate-related exposure. When a utility such as PG&E publicly outlines operational plans ahead of a storm, it provides additional information about how the company is managing ongoing system and safety challenges.

For investors following NYSE:PCG, these storm preparations provide another operational data point to watch over time, alongside capital spending plans, regulatory decisions, and progress on wildfire risk reduction. How efficiently PG&E executes during high-stress weather events, and how clearly it communicates with customers and regulators, can influence perceptions of its operational discipline and risk profile even before any financial results are reported.
